messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Focus on specific, measurable objectives linked to the call expected outcomes. Explain why the project is ambitious and beyond the current state of the art.
Write the Objectives and ambition section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.1 Objectives and ambition 

Introduction: Aligned with the scope of HORIZON-CL4-INDUSTRY-2025-01-TWIN-TRANSITION-37 Solving issues in carbon-neutral iron and steel making processes with diverse input materials of varying quality, our overall goal is to validate innovative low-CO₂ iron and steelmaking solutions by enabling the use of diverse, lower-quality biogenic residues in core steelmaking production routes—namely Blast Furnace–Basic Oxygen Furnace (BF-BOF), Electric Arc Furnace (EAF), and Direct Reduced Iron (DRI). We will develop industrially scalable processes for the production of bioanthracite and green hydrogen from biowastes and validate their controlled use as alternative reductants in BF-BOF, EAF and DRI, addressing their impact on process stability, product quality, and emissions reduction. 

We propose an advanced integrated system, based on innovative thermochemical processes, to convert biowastes into high-performance/quality biobased reductants, reducing production costs and ensuring high product sustainability by maximizing energy efficiency, and combining the recovery of biogenic carbon with the extraction of critical and strategic minerals, while also addressing the impact of replacing fossil-based reducing agents with biobased alternatives on input materials, processes, and the quality of the final iron and steel output. A range of heterogeneous feedstocks will be targeted including: (1) sewage and industrial sludge; the organic fraction of municipal solid waste (fresh, digested; digestate from manure and agricultural residues; contaminated urban biowaste; and various agri-food wastes, such as tomato waste, vinasse, and rice husk. Figure 1 provides a high-level schematic of our approach, whereby the technological routes involve Fast Hydrothermal treatment (HTT) to pretreat the biowaste, separating the biogenic carbon and elements in two main streams: a carbon-rich solid (hydrochar) and a liquid phase with high organic carbon content. In addition, two refinery lines will be investigated and scaled up for maximizing the valorisation of the biogenic carbon. A bioanthracite line aims to transform the solid (hydrochar) into a low-ash, high-quality bioanthracite, involving the following processes: 1) Chemical leaching of raw hydrochar to extract over 99% of calcium (Ca) phosphorus (P), reducing ash content; 2) Slow oxidative pyrolysis at temperatures from 600°C to 900°C to turn hydrochar into a stable, bioanthracite alkali-aided pyrolysis followed by hot washing to maximize the ash extraction inorganic content of feedstock with high Al and Si. A green gas line aims to transform the liquid phase into green hydrogen via three processes: 1) Supercritical Water Gasification (SCWG) of the HTT liquid to generate hydrogen-rich gas, with optional catalytic enhancement; 2) Anaerobic digestion (AD) to turn the biogenic organic carbon from the HTT liquid into biomethane, coupled with 3) Methane pyrolysis using bioanthracite as catalyst, to produce green hydrogen and to obtain C-enriched bioanthracite as a spent catalyst. 

The resulting bioanthracite and green hydrogen will be integrated into three Low-CO2 production routes:
- Coupling H2-based direct iron reduction with EAF using biobased gas & biogenic anthracite to produce pig iron
- Replacing coal in scrap metal EAF and BF by biogenic anthracite (bioanthracite)
- Replacing coke and SiO2 in silico-manganese alloy production by producing a C-SiO2 rich bioanthracite from sewage sludge.

Our research will address the system-level challenges in these routes, optimising and validating the process adaptations required for stable operation, emissions reduction, and consistent product quality under variable feedstock conditions. The integration of the three routes in steelmaking sites will also be investigated, in order to maximize the energy efficiency of the new Low-CO2 production processes. This will be supported by a modular digital intelligence layer (HoloSteel) combining Process Analytical Technologies (PAT), Quality by Design (QbD), and a holonic agent-based digital twin enabling advanced monitoring, characterisation, and control to ensure system-level reliability, product consistency, and operational resilience. This introduces a strong Industry 4.0 dimension, whereby HoloSteel will enable real-time monitoring, simulation, and predictive control to manage feedstock variability and support optimal decision-making at plant level, while enhancing transparency, responsiveness, and system resilience across steelmaking operations transitioning to low-CO₂ pathways. 

An Industry 5.0 approach complements this by embedding human-centric innovation, skills development, and strategic capacity-building. This includes the co-creation of new operational roles and targeted training programmes with input from plant operators, industrial partners, and social stakeholders, ensuring alignment with real-world needs. Both technical operators and business decision-makers will be equipped with the knowledge and tools required to manage process variability, digital interfaces, and alternative feedstock strategies. Business decision-makers will benefit from interpretable data visualisation and scenario-based modelling provided by HoloSteel, supporting investment planning and strategic oversight. A robust business case will underpin our proposed low-CO₂ process routes evaluating techno-economic performance, operational risk, and market adoption scenarios. Environmental performance will be systematically assessed. This holistic approach (combining technology, workforce evolution and strategic foresight) ensures steelmaking decarbonisation is not only technically feasible, but also economically viable and socially sustainable. Combined, these innovations represent a viable pathway to reduce dependence on fossil-based reductants, with the potential to cut greenhouse gas emissions by up to 24 Mt CO₂ annually by 2050.

Figure 1- High-level schematic of our systems-level approach
Our objectives: Our interdisciplinary team of world-class scientists, researchers, tech companies, industry actors and stakeholders have co-designed a series of objectives for delivering the call topic ambition of ‘Solving issues in carbon-neutral iron and steel making processes with diverse input materials of varying quality’. In close analysis of the call topic scope, we have taken care to formulate these objectives as S.M.A.R.T. (specific, measurable, attainable, realistic and timebound) and to be fulfilled within a 48-month timeline.

Table 1- Our S.M.A.R.T. objectives
Our objectives alignment with scope of HORIZON-CL4-INDUSTRY-2025-01-TWIN-TRANSITION-37: We offer a robust and system-oriented response to the challenges outlined in the call topic, targeting a reliable transition to carbon-neutral iron and steelmaking. We integrate material, process, digital and human-centric innovations across three major steelmaking routes (BF-BOF, EAF, and DRI) while validating the use of biogenic reductants and energy efficiency. At its core, we address key systemic constraints in low-CO₂ steelmaking, i.e. the increasing heterogeneity of input materials and the resulting need for adaptive, quality-assured process control. Through HoloSteel, a modular digital intelligence layer integrating PAT, QbD principles, and a holonic agent-based digital twin, we introduce an intelligent digital architecture that enables real-time process stability & system-wide predictability under highly variable feedstock conditions. 

We fully meet four of the five Expected Outcomes (discussed in detail in Section 2.1), addressing process variability, input heterogeneity, product quality assurance, and system-level adaptation across multiple production routes. Three distinct low-CO₂ pathways are addressed, including novel uses of bio-based carbon and synergies between direct reduction, scrap melting, and ferroalloy production. These pilots ensure strong technological anchoring and practical relevance. The call topic scope requirements are also comprehensively addressed, through our integrated, scalable, and human-centred approach, which positions us in strong alignment with the call topic’s technical, industrial, digital, and societal ambitions. We address 8 of the 10 Scope points: extending operational windows and de-risking low-CO₂ technologies, managing heterogeneity in feedstocks and reductants, ensuring high product quality from low-quality inputs, characterising material and process interactions through inline PAT and multi-scale analysis, coupling plant design with digital risk mitigation, enabling FAIR-compliant data integration for modelling, and embedding lifecycle assessment and costing for benchmarking. 

We also address two out of the three multidisciplinary research activities required under the topic. Firstly, we integrate advanced sensing, multi-scale characterisation, and agent-based modelling within a cohesive digital framework. Through a PAT sensor platform and the HoloSteel architecture, we enable real-time data acquisition, predictive control, and simulation across diverse steelmaking routes, integrating Machine Learning and agent-based models within the HoloSteel digital twin to enable predictive control, simulation, and system understanding (embodying an Industry 4.0 approach). These activities contribute directly to bridging physical process variability with digital intelligence, in line with the multidisciplinary R&D ambition of the topic. Secondly, we develop innovative, low-cost on-site hydrogen production concepts, using bio-based liquid streams and thermochemical pathways (e.g. supercritical water gasification and methane pyrolysis), supporting decentralised green hydrogen integration into steelmaking. This multidisciplinary depth strengthens our capacity to enable resilient, low-CO₂ production under variable feedstock and energy scenarios. We respond directly to the call’s emphasis on resilience, energy efficiency, substitution strategies, and techno-economic viability, underpinned by a dedicated business case and exploitation strategy. We introduce a forward-looking Industry 5.0 dimension. Through co-creation with plant operators and stakeholders, new operational roles and training programmes will be designed, equipping both technical staff and business decision-makers to manage variability, digital workflows, and strategic transitions. These human-centred innovations will be
